sourceQuestion:
A critical section is a program segment
A. which should run in a certain amount of time
B. which avoids deadlocks
C. where shared resources are accessed
D. which must be enclosed by a pair of semaphore operations, P and V
sourceAnswer: C. where shared resources are accessed

parsed.stem: A critical section is a program segment

options.text: which should run in a certain amount of time
which avoids deadlocks
where shared resources are accessed
which must be enclosed by a pair of semaphore operations, P and V

sourceQuestion:
A solution to the Dining Philosophers Problem which avoids deadlock is to
A. ensure that all philosophers pick up the left fork before the right fork
B. ensure that all philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork
C. ensure that one particular philosopher picks up the left fork before the right fork, and that all other philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: C. ensure that one particular philosopher picks up the left fork before the right fork, and that all other philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork

parsed.stem: A solution to the Dining Philosophers Problem which avoids deadlock is to

options.text: ensure that all philosophers pick up the left fork before the right fork
ensure that all philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork
ensure that one particular philosopher picks up the left fork before the right fork, and that all other philosophers pick up the right fork before the left fork
None of the above

sourceQuestion:
The concurrent programming constructs fork and join are as below:
Fork <label>: creates a new process executing from the specified label
Join <variable>: decrements the specified synchronization variable (by 1) and terminates the process if the new value is not 0.

Show the precedence graph for S1, S2, S3, S4, and S5 of the concurrent program below.

fn a
  S1
  fork L1
  S2
  fork L2
  S3
  join N
  S5
L1: S4
  join N
L2: (reached by S2's fork)

The concurrent program has statements S1 through S5. N is initialized to 2.

Which of the following precedence graphs correctly represents the execution order?
A. S1 -> S2 -> S3 -> S5, S1 -> S4, S2 -> S4
B. S1 -> S2, S1 -> S4, S2 -> S3 -> S5, S2 -> S4
C. S1 -> S2 -> S3, S1 -> S4, S3 and S4 both must complete before S5
D. S1 -> S2 -> S3, S1 -> S2 -> S4, S3 and S4 both before S5
sourceAnswer: C. S1 -> S2 -> S3, S1 -> S4, S3 and S4 both must complete before S5

parsed.stem:
The concurrent programming constructs fork and join are as below:
Fork <label>: creates a new process executing from the specified label
Join <variable>: decrements the specified synchronization variable (by 1) and terminates the process if the new value is not 0.

Show the precedence graph for S1, S2, S3, S4, and S5 of the concurrent program below.

fn a
  S1
  fork L1
  S2
  fork L2
  S3
  join N
  S5
L1: S4
  join N
L2: (reached by S2's fork)

The concurrent program has statements S1 through S5. N is initialized to 2.

Which of the following precedence graphs correctly represents the execution order?

options.text: S1 -> S2 -> S3, S1 -> S4, S3 and S4 both must complete before S5
S1 -> S2 -> S3, S1 -> S2 -> S4, S3 and S4 both before S5

sourceQuestion:
Each process P_i, i = 1 ... 9 is coded as follows:
repeat
  P(mutex)
  {Critical section}
  V(mutex)
forever

The code for P_10 is identical except it uses V(mutex) in place of P(mutex). What is the largest number of processes that can be inside the critical section at any moment?

A. 1    B. 2    C. 3    D. None

parsed.stem:
Each process P_i, i = 1 ... 9 is coded as follows:
repeat
  P(mutex)
  {Critical section}
  V(mutex)
forever

The code for P_10 is identical except it uses V(mutex) in place of P(mutex). What is the largest number of processes that can be inside the critical section at any moment?

options.text: None

sourceAnswer: A. Set busy=false and signal the highest priority waiting process

options.text: Set busy=false and signal the highest priority waiting process
Simply call V(mutex) to release mutual exclusion
Set R_requested[priority]=false and V(process[priority])
Busy-wait until no process is requesting R, then set busy=false

sourceQuestion:
When the result of a computation depends on the speed of the processes involved, there is said to be

A. cycle stealing
B. race condition
C. a time lock
D. a deadlock
sourceAnswer: B. race condition

parsed.stem: When the result of a computation depends on the speed of the processes involved, there is said to be

options.text: cycle stealing
race condition
a time lock
a deadlock

sourceAnswer: A. Yes, both processes can sleep simultaneously due to a race condition

options.text: Yes, both processes can sleep simultaneously due to a race condition
No, the atomic test-and-assign prevents both from sleeping
Only the producer can sleep
Only the consumer can sleep

sourceQuestion:
Let m[0], ..., m[3] be mutexes (binary semaphores) and P[0], ..., P[3] be processes. Suppose each process P[i] executes the following:

wait(m[i]); wait(m[(i+1) mod 4]);
...........
release(m[i]); release(m[(i+1) mod 4]);

This could cause:
A. Thrashing
B. Deadlock
C. Starvation, but not deadlock
D. None of the above
sourceAnswer: B. Deadlock

options.text: Thrashing
Deadlock
Starvation, but not deadlock
None of the above

sourceAnswer: A. Box 1: wait(mutex); Box 2: signal(mutex); Box 3: wait(mutex); starvation of writers is possible

options.text: Box 1: wait(mutex); Box 2: signal(mutex); Box 3: wait(mutex); starvation of writers is possible
Box 1: signal(mutex); Box 2: signal(mutex); Box 3: wait(mutex); starvation is not possible
Box 1: wait(mutex); Box 2: wait(mutex); Box 3: signal(mutex); starvation is not possible
Box 1: signal(mutex); Box 2: wait(mutex); Box 3: signal(mutex); starvation of writers is possible

sourceAnswer: A. flag[j] = true and turn = j

options.text: flag[j] = true and turn = j
flag[j] = true and turn = i
flag[j] = false and turn = j
flag[j] = false and turn = i

sourceAnswer: A. enter: while(TEST_AND_SET(&mutex) == 1); leave: mutex = 0;

options.text: enter: while(TEST_AND_SET(&mutex) == 1); leave: mutex = 0;
enter: while(TEST_AND_SET(&mutex) == 0); leave: mutex = 1;
enter: while(TEST_AND_SET(&mutex) != 0); leave: mutex = -1;
enter: while(mutex == 1); leave: mutex = 0;

sourceQuestion:
Suppose we want to synchronize two concurrent processes P and Q using binary semaphores S and T. The code for the processes P and Q is shown below.

Process P:          Process Q:
while(1){           while(1){
  W:                  Y:
  print '0';          print '1';
  print '0';          print '1';
  X:                  Z:
}

Synchronization statements can be inserted only at points W, X, Y, and Z.

Which of the following will ensure that the output string never contains a substring of the form 0011...0011?

A. P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 0
B. P(S) at W, V(T) at X, P(T) at Y, V(S) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 0
C. P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 0, T initially 1
D. P(S) at W, V(T) at X, P(T) at Y, V(S) at Z, S initially 0, T initially 1
sourceAnswer: B. P(S) at W, V(T) at X, P(T) at Y, V(S) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 0

options.text: P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 0
P(S) at W, V(T) at X, P(T) at Y, V(S) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 0
P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 0, T initially 1
P(S) at W, V(T) at X, P(T) at Y, V(S) at Z, S initially 0, T initially 1

sourceQuestion:
Suppose we want to synchronize two concurrent processes P and Q using binary semaphores S and T. The code for the processes P and Q is shown below.

Process P:          Process Q:
while(1){           while(1){
  W:                  Y:
  print '0';          print '1';
  print '0';          print '1';
  X:                  Z:
}

Synchronization statements can be inserted only at points W, X, Y, and Z.

Which of the following will ensure that the output string never contains a substring of the form 0^1*0 (i.e., a '0' followed by some '1's followed by '0') or 1^0*1 (i.e., a '1' followed by some '0's followed by '1')?

A. P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 1
B. P(S) at W, V(T) at X, P(T) at Y, V(S) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 0
C. P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 0, T initially 1

sourceAnswer: A. P(S) at W, V(S) at X, P(T) at Y, V(T) at Z, S initially 1, T initially 1

sourceQuestion:
Consider the methods used by processes P1 and P2 for accessing their critical sections whenever needed, as given below. The initial values of shared boolean variables S1 and S2 are randomly assigned.

Method used by P1:
while (S1 == S2);
Critical Section
S1 = S2;

Method used by P2:
while (S1 != S2);
Critical Section
S2 = not(S1);

Which one of the following statements describes the properties achieved?
A. Mutual exclusion but not progress
B. Progress but not mutual exclusion
C. Neither mutual exclusion nor progress
D. Both mutual exclusion and progress
sourceAnswer: A. Mutual exclusion but not progress

options.text: Mutual exclusion but not progress
Progress but not mutual exclusion
Neither mutual exclusion nor progress
Both mutual exclusion and progress

sourceAnswer: B. Exactly twice

options.text: At least twice
Exactly twice
Exactly thrice
Exactly once

sourceAnswer: B. fails as L can take on a non-zero value when the lock is actually available

fails as L can take on a non-zero value when the lock is actually available

sourceQuestion:
A shared variable x, initialized to zero, is operated on by four concurrent processes W, X, Y, Z as follows. Each of the processes W and X reads x from memory, increments by one, stores it to memory, and then terminates. Each of the processes Y and Z reads x from memory, decrements by two, stores it to memory, and then terminates. Each process before reading x invokes the P operation (i.e., wait) on a counting semaphore S and invokes the V operation (i.e., signal) on the semaphore S after storing x to memory. Semaphore S is initialized to two. What is the maximum possible value of x after all processes complete execution?

A. -2
B. -1
C. 1
D. 2

parsed.stem: A shared variable x, initialized to zero, is operated on by four concurrent processes W, X, Y, Z as follows. Each of the processes W and X reads x from memory, increments by one, stores it to memory, and then terminates. Each process before reading x invokes the P operation (i.e., wait) on a counting semaphore S and invokes the V operation (i.e., signal) on the semaphore S after storing x to memory. Semaphore S is initialized to two. What is the maximum possible value of x after all processes complete execution?

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two-process synchronization solution.

PROCESS 0
Entry: loop while (turn == 1);
       (critical section)
Exit:  turn = 1;

Process 1
Entry: loop while (turn == 0);
       (critical section)
Exit:  turn = 0;

The shared variable turn is initialized to zero. Which one of the following is TRUE?

A. This is a correct two-process synchronization solution.
B. The bounded wait condition is violated.
C. The progress requirement is violated.
D. The mutual exclusion requirement is violated.
sourceAnswer: C. The progress requirement is violated.

options.text: This is a correct two-process synchronization solution.
The bounded wait condition is violated.
The progress requirement is violated.
The mutual exclusion requirement is violated.

sourceQuestion:
A multithreaded program P executes with x number of threads and uses y number of locks for ensuring mutual exclusion while operating on shared memory locations. All locks in the program are non-reentrant, i.e., if a thread holds a lock l, then it cannot re-acquire lock l without releasing it. If a thread is unable to acquire a lock, it blocks until the lock becomes available. The minimum value of x and the minimum value of y together for which execution of P can result in a deadlock are:
A. x = 1, y = 2
B. x = 2, y = 1
C. x = 2, y = 2
D. x = 1, y = 1

parsed.stem: A multithreaded program P executes with x number of threads and uses y number of locks for ensuring mutual exclusion while operating on shared memory locations. All locks in the program are non-reentrant, i.e., if a thread holds a lock l, then it cannot re-acquire lock l without releasing it. If a thread is unable to acquire a lock, it blocks until the lock becomes available. The minimum value of x and the minimum value of y together for which execution of P can result in a deadlock are:

sourceAnswer: B. P : empty, Q : full, R : full, S : empty

options.text: P : full, Q : empty, R : empty, S : full
P : empty, Q : full, R : full, S : empty
P : empty, Q : full, R : empty, S : full
P : empty, Q : empty, R : full, S : full
